# Prior predictive checks

Before fitting anything, it pays to ask a blunt question:
**do my priors imply sensible data?** It is easy to write down a prior that looks
innocent and quietly believes in impossible fish. A prior predictive check catches
this by simulating
whole fake datasets from the priors *alone* — the real measurements never enter.

## How it works

1. Draw parameter values from their priors.
2. Push those values through the model to simulate a dataset.
3. Repeat many times and look at the spread of simulated data.

If your simulated "frogfish body lengths" come out spanning $-400$ to $600$ mm,
something is wrong: real frogfish are never negative, and a half-metre frogfish is
a monster. A prior that produces those values is putting belief on the impossible.
Tighten it.

## In FANGS

Use the **Prior Check** tab. It runs the model forward from the priors with the
likelihood switched off, so you see exactly what your assumptions predict *before*
the data gets any vote.

This is the natural partner to the $\sigma$-prior work you just did. A vague-looking
`dnorm(0, 1000)` on the mean body length seems harmless on the page — until the
prior check shows you the absurd fish it implies.

## Your task

Run a prior check on your frogfish body-length model. Then answer the question
below about how to read an obviously-too-vague prior.
